question.text: When interest rates rise, the market value of existing bonds typically:

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** Bond prices and interest rates have an inverse relationship. When interest rates rise, newly issued bonds offer higher coupon rates, making existing bonds with lower coupons less attractive. Consequently, the market value of existing bonds falls to adjust their effective yield upward to match new market rates.
**The Trap:** Some candidates confuse the impact on bond prices with bond yields. When rates rise, bond prices FALL but yields RISE. The relationship is inverse. Also, the degree of price change depends on the bond's duration -- longer duration bonds are more sensitive to rate changes.
**Cross-Exam Context:** This is the foundational concept behind interest rate risk in debt funds. SEBI categorizes debt funds partly based on duration (overnight, liquid, ultra-short, short, medium, long duration) because duration determines interest rate sensitivity. A gilt fund with long duration will fall sharply when rates rise.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Explain to clients that debt funds are NOT risk-free. When RBI raises repo rates, long-duration debt funds can deliver negative returns in the short term. Match the fund duration with the client's investment horizon to manage interest rate risk.
